summary: The post explains Fitts' Law, a fundamental principle of human-computer interaction stating that the time to reach a target depends on its distance and size. Atwood uses this to explain why Macintosh menus are faster than Windows menus—not because they're larger, but because they're pinned to the screen edge, making them effectively infinite in height. He explores the practical implications of screen edges as valuable UI real estate, while noting trade-offs like multi-monitor setups losing their natural edge boundaries. The post also critiques touchpad designs with dedicated scroll areas that destroy the usability benefits of natural edges.
key_points: Fitts' Law proves that target acquisition time is a function of distance and target size—a principle often ignored in UI design
Macintosh menus are faster than Windows menus because screen-edge placement makes them infinitely tall for Fitts' Law calculations
The four corners of a screen are the most quickly accessed targets due to their 'pinning action'
Multiple monitors eliminate natural screen edges, creating usability challenges that Fitts' Law predicts
Touchpads with dedicated scroll areas violate Fitts' Law by destroying the utility of natural touchpad edges
money_quotes: The time to acquire a target is a function of the distance to and size of the target.
Since the cursor stops at the edge of the screen, for the purposes of Fitts' law calculation, Macintosh menus are infinitely tall!
The edges could be your most valuable real estate. Use them responsibly.

Fitts' law indicates that the most quickly accessed targets on any computer display are the four corners of the screen, because of their pinning action, and yet, for years, they seemed to be avoided at all costs by designers.
On a touchpad with dedicated scrolling areas, you have no way to know when you've passed from touchpad area into the no-man's-land of scrolling area.
One unfortunate side-effect of multiple monitors is the removal of some natural edges between adjoining monitors.
key_insight: Screen edges are infinitely easy to target because cursors stop there, making them the most valuable real estate in UI design—yet designers routinely waste or destroy this advantage.
